name: calcom-validation
description: Exp 15: Validate framework-aware analysis, CO-CHANGE, and HOTSPOTS removal on cal.com (906K lines). Tests whether RISK filtering, CO-CHANGE section, and reduced token budget improve strand's signal quality on a large production monorepo.

questions.question: Which files are most dangerous to modify in cal.com's tRPC server layer? Explain why, citing specific evidence about their blast radius and downstream impact.

assertions.description: Identifies a high-risk tRPC server file
assertions.check: Response mentions at least one high-risk tRPC server file (errorFormatter.ts, sessionMiddleware.ts, or Prisma/database extensions) citing blast radius data such as amplification ratio, cascade depth, or affected file count
Response mentions sessionMiddleware.ts or session middleware as a high-risk file with broad downstream impact

assertions.description: Does NOT rank Playwright test helpers as production risk
assertions.check: Response does NOT list any Playwright files (playwright/lib/fixtures.ts, playwright/lib/loadJSON.ts, etc.) as dangerous production files. If Playwright files are mentioned, they should be explicitly noted as test infrastructure, not production risk.

questions.question: Rank these three files by how dangerous they are to modify in production, from most to least dangerous: (1) packages/trpc/server/errorFormatter.ts, (2) packages/emails/lib/sanitizeDisplayName.ts, (3) apps/web/playwright/lib/fixtures.ts. Explain your reasoning.

assertions.description: Ranks errorFormatter.ts as most or second-most dangerous
assertions.check: Response ranks errorFormatter.ts as #1 or #2 most dangerous, citing its amplification ratio, cascade depth, or affected file count

assertions.description: Ranks playwright/fixtures.ts as least dangerous in production
assertions.check: Response ranks playwright/lib/fixtures.ts as the LEAST dangerous for production, correctly identifying it as test infrastructure that does not affect production code
Response cites specific numbers for blast radius, amplification ratio, cascade depth, or affected file counts — not just vague statements about 'many dependencies'

questions.question: What is the blast radius of changing packages/platform/atoms/src/constants/styles.ts? Which modules and files would be affected?

assertions.check: Response mentions that changes cascade from the platform/atoms package into the apps/web module (cross-module impact)
Response mentions amplification ratio or that the change affects many files transitively, citing specific numbers from the encoding

questions.question: packages/trpc/server/types.ts is widely imported across the tRPC server layer. If it needs breaking changes to its exported types, what is the scope of impact? How would you approach this safely?

assertions.check: Response identifies that types.ts is imported by many files (dozens or more), ideally citing a specific count from the encoding data
Response suggests a staged or incremental approach to breaking changes (e.g., deprecation period, type aliases, gradual migration) rather than a single big-bang change

questions.question: Booking confirmations are failing in production. Based on the codebase structure and recent changes, which files would you investigate first and why?

assertions.description: References recently changed booking files from CHURN
assertions.check: Response mentions at least one of: BookingRepository.ts, handleCancelBooking.ts, RegularBookingService.ts, or BookingDetailsSheet.tsx — files that have been actively changed recently
Response mentions email-related files (renderEmail.ts, emails/src/templates, sanitizeDisplayName.ts) as relevant to booking confirmation failures

assertions.description: Uses temporal evidence (recent changes) not just structural
assertions.check: Response references recent commit activity, churn data, or 'recently modified' as part of the investigation rationale — not just static dependency analysis

questions.question: What files in this codebase frequently change together? Are there any surprising co-change patterns where files change together but don't directly import each other?

assertions.check: Response lists at least one specific pair of files that frequently change together, citing co-change count or frequency
Response distinguishes between files that change together AND import each other vs files that change together but have NO direct import relationship (the surprising signal)

questions.question: A pull request modifies packages/trpc/server/middlewares/sessionMiddleware.ts to change the isAuthed export. What should code reviewers pay special attention to? What is the risk of this change?

assertions.check: Response warns about the large downstream impact / blast radius of sessionMiddleware.ts, mentioning that it affects many files or has high amplification
Response mentions specific downstream files or patterns that depend on isAuthed (e.g., authedProcedure.ts, tRPC routers, protected routes)

assertions.description: Recommends testing scope proportional to blast radius
assertions.check: Response recommends broad testing, CI checks, or careful rollout given the wide blast radius — not just unit testing the middleware itself
